analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Aljamain Sterling -- Style & Profile

- Sterling is a grappling-first fighter who has built his UFC career on wrestling control and positional dominance, but his recent output tells a story of significant offensive decline. His career striking average of 4.26 significant strikes per minute is flatly contradicted by his last three fights, where he's landing 0.32, 1.08, and 0.20 per minute respectively — a dramatic, sustained drop that suggests either strategic conservatism or a fighter whose offensive tools have significantly diminished at 36.

- The striking accuracy collapse mirrors the volume drop. Career accuracy sits at 52%, but his last three fights show 33%, 61%, and 27% — with the 61% coming against Ortega in a slow, clinch-heavy affair where he barely threw. When he does commit to punching exchanges, he's not landing cleanly against good competition.

- His takedown output has also fallen off. Career average of 2.54 per 15 minutes, but his last two fights show 0.6 and 0.0. The Evloev loss exposed him as a fighter who can no longer impose his grappling consistently when opponents are well-prepared for it, and Zalal's first fight showed him holding Sterling to zero takedowns in response.

- Sterling has been stopped twice in his career — both by O'Malley (KO, Round 2) and Dillashaw (KO, which he reversed). He's 36 years old competing at featherweight, and the combination of age, a cracked chin signal from the O'Malley knockout, and the visible offensive decline makes him a fade candidate in close fights. He has not won a fight by finish since 2022.

- He fights smart and patiently, but that patience has increasingly looked like reluctance. In both recent five-round decisions, he accumulated points through clinch work, tie-ups, and short bursts rather than sustained pressure — a style that is beatable on the scorecards by a fighter willing to stay active.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Youssef Zalal -- Style & Profile

- Zalal is a submission hunter first, with four of his last five wins coming by submission — all in the first two rounds. His career submission average of 1.3 attempts per 15 minutes understates how active he is on the ground when he actually gets there, but his 55% takedown defense and 31% takedown accuracy mean he's not going to outmuscle elite wrestlers to get to his preferred position.

- His striking volume is low (2.78 career SLpM, trending lower in recent fights), but his efficiency is real — 50% career accuracy and 64% strike defense are both meaningful numbers that suggest he doesn't flail and doesn't get hit often when the fight is in exchanges. He's a counterpuncher who is comfortable fighting at range and staying disciplined.

- The switch-stance element adds genuine complexity. He can threaten from both sides and changes looks in a way that disrupts timing-dependent fighters. Against Calvin Kattar, he used it effectively to navigate a strong boxer over three rounds for a clear decision.

- His win over Josh Emmett is the most instructive fight in his recent run — he got a takedown early, controlled, and finished a dangerous, experienced featherweight in 98 seconds. Against a fighter with Emmett's power, his willingness to be first to the floor and finish from top is a real weapon.

- The first Sterling fight was a competitive but ultimately ineffective performance. He landed zero takedowns and threw almost nothing (0.16 SLpM), which tells you Sterling completely neutralized his grappling threat and Zalal had no fallback plan when his submission game was taken away.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This rematch is a fascinating study in whether a 29-year-old can solve a puzzle he already couldn't crack once, or whether a 36-year-old who barely won the first fight has enough left to grind through another five rounds. Sterling won the original fight on the judges' scorecards in a genuinely uneventful five rounds — he out-pointed Zalal by doing less, which is a remarkable thing to say about a fighter who used to be one of the most active competitors in the bantamweight division.

The central question in this fight is identical to the first: can Zalal get it to the ground? Sterling's takedown defense sits at 44% career-wide, which is poor on paper, but in the last two fights he held up well enough to stay vertical. If Zalal can't shoot successfully, he's left throwing 0.16 strikes per minute and hoping for an accident. His striking game is competent but passive — he's not going to outpoint Sterling in a stand-up chess match when Sterling is content to eat the round doing nothing. Conversely, if Zalal can pressure Sterling into the fence and initiate the wrestling battle from clinch entries rather than pure takedown shots, his submission threat from top position becomes real. Sterling has never been finished by submission, but he hasn't faced anyone as focused on that path as Zalal in his recent run.

Sterling's age and the visible decline in every offensive category he owns are the most important factors. He's 36, seven years older than Zalal, and the trajectory of his last three fights — particularly the Evloev loss and the hollow win over Zalal in Fight 1 — points to a fighter running on experience and game planning rather than physical tools. In a five-round fight, Zalal's 29-year-old engine is a real asset if he can stay disciplined and force action rather than waiting to react. The net intangible picture strongly favors Zalal on age and trajectory, though Sterling's experience in championship rounds cannot be entirely discounted. The fighter who imposes pace in rounds three through five will almost certainly win.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 — Youssef Zalal wins by unanimous decision, Round 5**
Likelihood: High
Why: Zalal learned from the first fight and comes with a more aggressive pace, forcing exchanges and winning the activity battle as Sterling's 36-year-old engine fades in the championship rounds. Sterling's recent output of 0.32 and 0.20 strikes per minute in five-rounders is not a winning strategy against a motivated 29-year-old who fights smart. Zalal doesn't need a finish — he just needs to do more than Sterling, which is a low bar based on recent evidence.

**#2 — Aljamain Sterling wins by unanimous decision, Round 5**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Sterling already proved once that he can neutralize everything Zalal brings and win a five-round decision through patience, clinch work, and just enough takedown activity to show the judges control. If Zalal again fails to land a takedown and throws at the same anemic volume as Fight 1, Sterling's experience in championship rounds and his superior grappling pedigree carries him on the cards for a second time.

**#3 — Youssef Zalal wins by submission, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Zalal's most dangerous version of himself materializes when he can get a takedown early and work from top position — see the Emmett finish for what that looks like in real time. Sterling's takedown defense has been inconsistent, and if Zalal catches him early with a level change before Sterling's game plan is fully locked in, a rear-naked choke or guillotine is a real outcome. The submission finish probability is tempered by Sterling's durability in this area across his entire career.

**#4 — Split decision either fighter**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Given how close and uneventful the first fight was, a split decision outcome is genuinely on the table regardless of who gets the nod. If the pattern from Fight 1 repeats — minimal striking, minimal grappling, five rounds of positioning — one judge going the other way is plausible. This is effectively the "the fight is too close to call and the judges disagree" outcome, which is a real possibility when two technically sound, offense-averse fighters share a cage for 25 minutes.
analysis_sections.betting:
**Tier: LEAN — Youssef Zalal**

The line movement here is the first thing that stands out: Sterling opened at -155 and has since moved to -105, while Zalal shifted from +115 to +135. Both lines lengthened — a sign of two-way action rather than a clean sharp signal in either direction. There is no reverse line movement here, so the market shift doesn't override the analytical read, but it does confirm that money has come in on Zalal and Sterling is no longer a meaningful favorite at this price.

Sterling at -105 implies the market believes this fight is essentially a coin flip, and the de-vigged true probability on each side sits right around 50/50. That framing actually makes Zalal the value play — he's the younger fighter, he's 7 years Sterling's junior, he's fought at a higher pace in his best recent outings, and Sterling's last three fight performances show a fighter who is running on empty offensively. Zalal at +135 implies he wins roughly 43% of the time in the market's assessment. Given what we know about Sterling's declining output and Zalal's physical prime, that feels like the wrong number by a margin worth acting on — lightly.

The hesitation comes from what already happened once. Zalal had Sterling in front of him for five full rounds and produced 0.16 strikes per minute and zero takedowns. If he shows up with the same passivity, Sterling wins again, and there's no strong evidence from the data that Zalal has materially upgraded his approach. This is not a confident value play — it is a lean based on age trajectory, line value at +135, and the expectation that Zalal adjusts at least somewhat from the first encounter.

**Pick:** Zalal +135
**Confidence:** 54%
**Stake:** 0.5 units
**Target line:** No worse than +110 for this to retain value

**Data notes:** Both fighters have full UFC records and recent activity. Sterling's layoff is listed as 6 days — this almost certainly reflects that this is a rematch recorded shortly after Fight 1, meaning both fighters are coming off the same fight with identical preparation context.

**Market read:** Sterling opened -155, now sits near -105. Zalal opened +115, now +135. Movement on both sides lengthened, suggesting two-way action without a clear sharp directional signal. No reverse line movement is present.

**Hedge verdict: NO ARB.** The lines on both sides do not combine below 100% implied probability at any available price. Take the value pick on Zalal at the best available line. Do not hedge.

**Props worth considering:** The "fight goes to decision" YES is worth examining. Both fighters have produced nothing but five-round decisions in their recent outputs, neither has finished the other in five rounds of prior competition, and Zalal's submission threat requires a takedown he couldn't land in Fight 1. Decision probability in this matchup is high — if that prop is available at a price implying less than 70% likelihood, it has value.

**Flip conditions:** If Zalal's camp confirms he has done no meaningful wrestling adjustments since Fight 1, push to no bet. If Sterling shows at weigh-ins looking physically compromised or underweight, the Zalal play strengthens. If the line moves past Zalal -110 before fight time, the value is gone regardless of the read.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Joselyne Edwards -- Style & Profile

- Edwards is an aggressive, forward-moving striker who works best when she can impose pace and volume. Her career 4.61 significant strikes per minute is well above average for the division, and she backs it up with 53% accuracy — she's not just throwing, she's landing.
- Her three-fight win streak shows striking versatility: a KO finish against Cachoeira (10.83 strikes per minute in that fight — she turned the pressure all the way up), a submission win over Cornolle, and a decision win over Dumont. She can finish fights across multiple methods.
- Her grappling is limited — 1.29 takedowns per 15 minutes, 36% accuracy, and zero takedown attempts in each of her last three fights. She wins with her hands and occasionally hunts submissions off scrambles, but she's not dragging people down.
- She absorbs 3.26 strikes per minute against a career average, and her 50% strike defense is below average for the division. She wins exchanges, but she doesn't avoid punishment to do it.
- Five career weight misses are a notable flag. In her most recent fight against Dumont, her output cratered to 0.4 strikes per minute — far below her norm — suggesting the rematch was a tactically conservative performance rather than a typical Edwards showing.
- At 30, she's in her prime physical window, and her five-fight win streak (including wins over Cornolle and Dumont in back-to-back fights after losses to both) speaks to real development in her game.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Norma Dumont -- Style & Profile

- Dumont is a distance-manager who wins on output and ring generalship rather than power or finishing ability. She's 8 decisions in her last 10 fights, with zero KO or submission wins in that stretch. She controls fights; she doesn't end them.
- Her 64% strike defense is the most important number in her profile — she's excellent at not getting hit, which is how she survives and thrives in close fights. Her 2.33 strikes absorbed per minute is among the better marks in the division.
- She has 54% takedown accuracy (career), but she attempted zero takedowns in each of her last three fights, including two against top-15 opponents. Her grappling stats look better than her actual fight behavior suggests.
- At 35, she's past the age where women in this division typically maintain their top-end performance. That doesn't mean she's shot, but it does mean her already-limited ceiling is lowering, not raising.
- Five career weight misses — matching Edwards — and the same six-day turnaround here. She came into the first fight at a weight disadvantage, and nothing suggests that's changed for the rematch.
- She already lost to Edwards once, on April 25th — six days ago. This is an immediate rematch on the same card or within the same week. That's an extraordinarily short window to adjust a game plan and come back and win.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The fundamental tension in this matchup is Edwards' volume and finishing ability against Dumont's discipline and defensive shell. Dumont's 64% strike defense is legitimately elite, and it explains why she controlled so many of her fights over the past few years — opponents simply couldn't land enough clean to damage her. But Edwards already solved that puzzle six days ago. She put Dumont on the mat and outworked her over three rounds to a unanimous decision. The defensive numbers that make Dumont dangerous against most opponents didn't stop Edwards, and there is no reason to believe a six-day turnaround produces a meaningful tactical adjustment.

Edwards' weakness is her own chin and strike defense — at 50%, she absorbs punishment in a way that would concern you against a finisher. But Dumont has zero KO wins in her last ten fights and a 0.0 submission average. She doesn't threaten from that angle. Dumont's path to winning this rematch runs almost entirely through outscoring Edwards on the scorecards, landing cleaner and more often across three rounds. Against an opponent who already beat her doing exactly the things Dumont needs to stop, that's an uphill road. Edwards also holds a three-inch reach advantage, which matters for controlling distance when Dumont tries to close and work behind her jab.

The net intangible picture doesn't favor either fighter strongly — both have weight-miss histories, both had identical six-day layoffs, and both share the same compressed turnaround. But the momentum of the result from six days ago sits entirely with Edwards. Dumont needs something to be different this time; the burden of proof is on her.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Joselyne Edwards wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: High
Why: The first fight went to a unanimous decision, and the style dynamics haven't changed in six days. Edwards likely controls pace and volume, Dumont defends well enough to survive but can't generate the offensive output to take rounds convincingly. Given neither fighter is a finisher in this matchup, the judges decide it again.

**#2 -- Norma Dumont wins by split or unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Dumont's disciplined defense allows her to absorb and counter, and if she adjusts her ring cutting and presses Edwards into the fence more aggressively than she did six days ago, she can steal rounds. The first fight was close enough to flip — a split decision either way would not be a shock.

**#3 -- Joselyne Edwards wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Edwards has finished fights inside two rounds recently — both the Cachoeira and Chandler KOs came in the first round. If Dumont's output and urgency improve in early rounds (knowing she needs to do more than the first fight), she may walk into something. But Dumont's defensive discipline makes this unlikely.

**Primary Pick: Joselyne Edwards**
**Confidence: 57%**

Edwards won this fight six days ago on a unanimous decision. The line on her has actually shortened slightly since opening (she opened at +170 and is currently around +165), while Dumont has moved from -195 to -200 — the market has barely moved and there's no reverse line movement signal pointing sharply in either direction. The sharp money, such as it is, has very mildly followed Dumont, likely on the basis of name recognition and record. Market data is limited here, so the read is almost entirely matchup-driven.

Dumont at -200 implies the market believes she wins roughly 67% of the time. Given that Edwards already beat her once — six days ago, on the same kind of 3-round undercard format — that number is hard to justify. Edwards at +165 implies she wins about 38% of the time. The de-vigged true probability sits closer to 37% for Edwards, against an assessed probability I'd put at 54–57%. That's a lean, not a conviction, but it's real value at the current price.

The reason this doesn't become a full play is the 6-day turnaround for both fighters and the inherently limited information in a same-week immediate rematch. Sometimes a fighter comes back sharper with a specific adjustment, and Dumont is disciplined enough to have one. The first fight's stats were so suppressed on both sides — Edwards landed at 0.4 strikes per minute, well below her 4.61 career average — that it's genuinely difficult to know what we're actually watching tactically.

**Stake: 1.0 unit**
**Target line: +140 or better**
If the line shortens past +140 on Edwards, the value narrows to the point where it's not worth taking. At +165, it's a clear lean.

**Data tiers:** Edwards — solid UFC sample with the most recent fight being the fight itself (though one-fight last data shouldn't override career context). Dumont — same. Both are medium-confidence assessments given the unusual immediate-rematch context.

**Market read:** No reverse line movement. Dumont shortened very slightly from -195 to -200; Edwards shortened from +170 to +165. Movement is minimal and directionally flat — no strong sharp signal either way.

**Hedge verdict: NO ARB.** Best available line on Edwards is approximately +165. Best available line on Dumont is approximately -200 (implied ~66.7%). Combined implied probability is roughly 104%. No arbitrage opportunity exists. Take the value pick on Edwards at the best available number.

**Props:** No prop value identified. Both fighters went to the scorecards in the first meeting, but the sample is too small and the lines on decision props are priced to reflect the history. Without a clear finishing threat on either side and no available prop line data, no props are recommended.

But Edwards already solved that puzzle six days ago. She put Dumont on the mat and outworked her over three rounds to a unanimous decision. The defensive numbers that make Dumont dangerous against most opponents didn't stop Edwards, and there is no reason to believe a six-day turnaround produces a meaningful tactical adjustment.

Edwards' weakness is her own chin and strike defense — at 50%, she absorbs punishment in a way that would concern you against a finisher. But Dumont has zero KO wins in her last ten fights and a 0.0 submission average. She doesn't threaten from that angle. Dumont's path to winning this rematch runs almost entirely through outscoring Edwards on the scorecards, landing cleaner and more often across three rounds. Against an opponent who already beat her doing exactly the things Dumont needs to stop, that's an uphill road. Edwards also holds a three-inch reach advantage, which matters for controlling distance when Dumont tries to close and work behind her jab.

The net intangible picture doesn't favor either fighter strongly — both have weight-miss histories, both had identical six-day layoffs, and both share the same compressed turnaround. ## Rafa Garcia -- Style & Profile

- Garcia is a pressure-forward wrestler who wins by making fights ugly — he grinds opponents into the fence, mixes in takedowns, and accumulates control time and strike volume across three rounds. His career 3.22 takedowns per 15 minutes and 77% takedown defense are the structural pillars of his game.
- His last three fights all ended in the third round — two decisions and a KO — and all three of his most recent outings were wins. He's on a three-fight winning streak that includes a KO of Jared Gordon and a decision over Hernandez in their first meeting.
- The striking numbers tell a cautious story: 4.28 landed per minute career-wide, but just 0.67 in the Hernandez fight and 0.8 against Pichel. He doesn't need to be flashy on the feet — he uses the threat of the takedown to dictate range and disrupt rhythm.
- He absorbed a second-round KO loss to Grant Dawson before this current run, which is the one blemish on his recent record. Dawson took him down and finished him from top position with strikes — a reminder that Garcia's wrestling is his foundation but it can be beaten by elite-level grapplers with power.
- At 31 with no weight-cut issues flagged and consistent activity, Garcia looks like a fighter in the prime of his career. His last three performances have been controlled, professional wins against legitimate lightweight competition.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Alexander Hernandez -- Style & Profile

- Hernandez is a striking-first lightweight whose two biggest weapons are his volume output and his power — he knocked out Chase Hooper in Round 1 and finished Diego Ferreira in Round 2, both by TKO, showing he can close the show when he lands clean.
- The problem is his striking defense, which sits at 59% career-wide and has shown no improvement in recent fights. He absorbs 4.45 significant strikes per minute — essentially a mirror image of Garcia's own absorption rate — and his last-3 accuracy of 29–33% in losses suggests he struggles to land cleanly against fighters who stay disciplined defensively.
- His takedown defense is a real vulnerability at 68% career-wide, and in the first fight with Garcia he recorded zero takedowns and was clearly smothered by Garcia's pressure. If he can't create separation or stuff the shots, his striking game simply doesn't get to happen.
- He's 33 years old with a 9-fight loss column that includes stoppages by Renato Moicano (rear-naked choke, R2), Billy Quarantillo (TKO, R2), and a split-decision loss to Damon Jackson. The variety of ways he's been beaten suggests a fighter who can look excellent against the right opponent and structurally outmatched against the wrong one.
- One weight miss on his record is a flag worth noting — not alarming as a single instance, but combined with his age and the nature of a rematch fight, it's worth tracking whether any late or post-weigh-in movement materializes.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

We don't have to guess how this fight plays out — we've already seen it. Garcia defeated Hernandez by unanimous decision on April 25, 2026, and the splits from that fight tell the whole story: Garcia landed 0.67 significant strikes per minute to Hernandez's 0.53, Garcia attempted takedowns while Hernandez attempted zero, and Hernandez connected on less than 30% of his strikes. Garcia controlled the pace, controlled the cage, and won every round. Now they're doing it again six days later, presumably as a short-notice rematch.

The structural reasons Garcia won the first fight haven't changed. His 77% takedown defense largely neutralizes anything Hernandez might try on the ground, while his 3.22 takedown average gives him a consistent way to drag the fight into positions where he thrives. Hernandez's 68% takedown defense is the master variable in this matchup — against a wrestler with Garcia's accuracy and aggression, that number projects a steady stream of successful shots across three rounds. Hernandez's path to winning essentially requires keeping this fight standing, creating distance, and landing the kind of early power shot that he showed against Hooper and Ferreira. His two-inch reach advantage is real but wasn't enough in the first meeting to keep Garcia from closing the distance.

Hernandez is a live underdog only in the scenario where he's made significant tactical adjustments since their last meeting six days ago — which, given the short turnaround, is nearly impossible. You don't overhaul a game plan in under a week. Garcia's pressure, cage work, and wrestling likely produce a nearly identical fight to the one we just watched.

## Davey Grant -- Style & Profile

- Grant is a 40-year-old bantamweight who has survived at UFC level longer than almost anyone his age in the division, but the last three fights tell a story of a fighter who has fundamentally shifted how he operates — his career striking rate of 5.58 per minute has cratered to roughly 2-3 per minute in his recent outings, suggesting he's fighting much more conservatively, relying on output management rather than volume.
- His accuracy has actually improved in recent fights (49-60% in last 3 vs. 45% career), which sounds positive until you notice he's only throwing when he's extremely confident — the tradeoff being that he's barely a presence offensively in many stretches of a fight.
- He has almost no grappling threat in his recent fights — 0.0 takedowns per 15 minutes across all three of his last fights, and a career submission rate of 0.2 per 15 minutes. His wins are coming from outworking opponents over 15 minutes, not from any specific finishing tool.
- The age signal here is real and significant. At 40, he is well past the cliff point for fighters in the lighter weight classes, and the strike defense sitting at 54% career (meaning he absorbs over 4 significant strikes per minute at full career pace) is a durability concern if an opponent can push a high output.
- The Jourdain submission loss sandwiched between two decisions over journeyman-level opposition is a mild red flag — he was finished in Round 1 by a sub, which is unusual for a fighter who rarely gets taken down, suggesting he was off-balance or badly hurt standing before the finish.
- He has beaten Martinetti once already — in the most recent fight on April 25, 2026 — so this is an immediate rematch with a six-day turnaround for both fighters.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Adrian Luna Martinetti -- Style & Profile

- Luna Martinetti has only two UFC fights on record and the career numbers are extremely volatile — 8.80 significant strikes landed per minute career average with 9.30 absorbed per minute, which sounds like a wild brawler, but both numbers likely reflect a single UFC debut skewing everything, since his last two fights came in at 1.2-2.87 SLpM.
- The 47% strike defense career average is genuinely concerning — absorbing nearly one of every two significant strikes thrown at you is a real problem, but with only two UFC fights in the sample, it's hard to know how predictive that number is going forward.
- His takedown defense is listed at 100%, but again this is from a two-fight sample with zero takedown attempts against him recorded — that's a statistical artifact, not a proven defensive skill. Treat it with skepticism.
- At 30 years old he has the age advantage in this rematch — a full decade younger than Grant — and at bantamweight that gap matters, particularly for pace and recovery.
- He lost the first meeting to Grant by unanimous decision in Round 3, which means he's now walking into a fight against someone who has already figured out a game plan that works against him. The question for this rematch is whether he's made genuine adjustments or is simply getting another shot at a familiar opponent.
- With only two UFC fights on record, his actual style and tendencies are genuinely unclear. The career average of 8.80 SLpM looks like a high-output brawler, but his last two fights show a much quieter, more conservative fighter. It is impossible to know which version shows up.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The most important fact in this matchup is that it already happened six days ago and Grant won. This isn't a rematch booked months later with time for adjustments — it's an immediate re-run with essentially the same physical and mental state for both men. Grant already solved this puzzle once. Luna Martinetti lost and now has to beat someone who just beat him, on the same week, without the ability to go back to camp and work on specific problems. That context shapes everything.

Grant's path to winning again is the same path that worked the first time: he fights conservatively, picks his shots (he landed at 49% accuracy in Fight 1 of this matchup), manages pace, and wins a grinding decision. His career output numbers are misleading because he clearly doesn't fight at that volume anymore — he's a counter-puncher at this stage, waiting for clean looks rather than forcing exchanges. Luna Martinetti's 47% strike defense means he gets tagged fairly often when he engages, which plays into Grant's pick-your-spot approach. The reach advantage Luna Martinetti holds (71" vs. Grant's 69") is real but modest — two inches doesn't fundamentally alter the range dynamics here.

Luna Martinetti's path to winning requires either a significant tactical adjustment from Fight 1 or catching Grant with something clean early. The problem is that at 1.2 SLpM in their first meeting, he was barely active offensively — he didn't lose because Grant overwhelmed him, he lost because he couldn't impose himself. For him to win the rematch he needs to be meaningfully more aggressive while somehow also defending better, which is a difficult combination to find in six days. Grant's age (40) is a legitimate fade concern in the abstract, but against this specific opponent he's already proven he can go 15 minutes effectively.

**#1 -- Davey Grant wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: High
Why: This is exactly how Fight 1 ended, and nothing in the rematch context suggests a different outcome. Grant fights conservatively, lands at solid accuracy, and outworks a passive Luna Martinetti over three rounds. The six-day turnaround gives Luna Martinetti almost no time to make meaningful tactical changes, and he showed in the first fight that he struggles to impose his offense consistently.

**#2 -- Adrian Luna Martinetti wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: If Luna Martinetti comes out with dramatically more forward pressure and forces Grant to deal with a higher volume than he's comfortable managing, the age and output factors could flip the decision. Grant at 40 can be outworked if someone actually pushes pace on him — his SLpM has dropped significantly, and a younger fighter willing to pour it on could take rounds by sheer activity.

**#3 -- Davey Grant wins by submission or TKO, Round 1-3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Grant has finished fights in his recent history (Assuncao sub, Smolka TKO), so the finishing ability is real but increasingly dormant. If Luna Martinetti's defense deteriorates under pressure or he makes an error in the clinch, Grant has the experience to capitalize. This scenario requires Luna Martinetti to be caught badly, which is possible given his 47% strike defense but not the expected path.

**#4 -- Adrian Luna Martinetti wins by finish**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Luna Martinetti has no UFC finishes and his recent fights have been very low-output decisions. Grant's chin has held up through a long career without a KO loss on record in this stretch. A finish for Martinetti would require a sequence that hasn't materialized yet against any opponent at this level.

At 40, he is well past the cliff point for fighters in the lighter weight classes, and the strike defense sitting at 54% career (meaning he absorbs over 4 significant strikes per minute at full career pace) is a durability concern if an opponent can push a high output.
- The Jourdain submission loss sandwiched between two decisions over journeyman-level opposition is a mild red flag — he was finished in Round 1 by a sub, which is unusual for a fighter who rarely gets taken down, suggesting he was off-balance or badly hurt standing before the finish.
- He has beaten Martinetti once already — in the most recent fight on April 25, 2026 — so this is an immediate rematch with a six-day turnaround for both fighters.
<!--END-->

<!--F2_PROFILE-->
## Adrian Luna Martinetti -- Style & Profile

- Luna Martinetti has only two UFC fights on record and the career numbers are extremely volatile — 8.80 significant strikes landed per minute career average with 9.30 absorbed per minute, which sounds like a wild brawler, but both numbers likely reflect a single UFC debut skewing everything, since his last two fights came in at 1.2-2.87 SLpM.
- The 47% strike defense career average is genuinely concerning — absorbing nearly one of every two significant strikes thrown at you is a real problem, but with only two UFC fights in the sample, it's hard to know how predictive that number is going forward.
- His takedown defense is listed at 100%, but again this is from a two-fight sample with zero takedown attempts against him recorded — that's a statistical artifact, not a proven defensive skill. Treat it with skepticism.
- At 30 years old he has the age advantage in this rematch — a full decade younger than Grant — and at bantamweight that gap matters, particularly for pace and recovery.
- He lost the first meeting to Grant by unanimous decision in Round 3, which means he's now walking into a fight against someone who has already figured out a game plan that works against him. The question for this rematch is whether he's made genuine adjustments or is simply getting another shot at a familiar opponent.
- With only two UFC fights on record, his actual style and tendencies are genuinely unclear. The career average of 8.80 SLpM looks like a high-output brawler, but his last two fights show a much quieter, more conservative fighter. It is impossible to know which version shows up.
<!--END-->

<!--HEAD2HEAD-->
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The most important fact in this matchup is that it already happened six days ago and Grant won. This isn't a rematch booked months later with time for adjustments — it's an immediate re-run with essentially the same physical and mental state for both men. Grant already solved this puzzle once. Luna Martinetti lost and now has to beat someone who just beat him, on the same week, without the ability to go back to camp and work on specific problems. That context shapes everything.

Grant's path to winning again is the same path that worked the first time: he fights conservatively, picks his shots (he landed at 49% accuracy in Fight 1 of this matchup), manages pace, and wins a grinding decision. His career output numbers are misleading because he clearly doesn't fight at that volume anymore — he's a counter-puncher at this stage, waiting for clean looks rather than forcing exchanges. Luna Martinetti's 47% strike defense means he gets tagged fairly often when he engages, which plays into Grant's pick-your-spot approach. The reach advantage Luna Martinetti holds (71" vs. Grant's 69") is real but modest — two inches doesn't fundamentally alter the range dynamics here.

Luna Martinetti's path to winning requires either a significant tactical adjustment from Fight 1 or catching Grant with something clean early. The problem is that at 1.2 SLpM in their first meeting, he was barely active offensively — he didn't lose because Grant overwhelmed him, he lost because he couldn't impose himself. For him to win the rematch he needs to be meaningfully more aggressive while somehow also defending better, which is a difficult combination to find in six days. Grant's age (40) is a legitimate fade concern in the abstract, but against this specific opponent he's already proven he can go 15 minutes effectively.

<!--END-->

<!--ENDINGS-->

Likelihood: High
Why: This is exactly how Fight 1 ended, and nothing in the rematch context suggests a different outcome. Grant fights conservatively, lands at solid accuracy, and outworks a passive Luna Martinetti over three rounds. The six-day turnaround gives Luna Martinetti almost no time to make meaningful tactical changes, and he showed in the first fight that he struggles to impose his offense consistently.

**#2 -- Adrian Luna Martinetti wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: If Luna Martinetti comes out with dramatically more forward pressure and forces Grant to deal with a higher volume than he's comfortable managing, the age and output factors could flip the decision. Grant at 40 can be outworked if someone actually pushes pace on him — his SLpM has dropped significantly, and a younger fighter willing to pour it on could take rounds by sheer activity.

**#3 -- Davey Grant wins by submission or TKO, Round 1-3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Grant has finished fights in his recent history (Assuncao sub, Smolka TKO), so the finishing ability is real but increasingly dormant. If Luna Martinetti's defense deteriorates under pressure or he makes an error in the clinch, Grant has the experience to capitalize. This scenario requires Luna Martinetti to be caught badly, which is possible given his 47% strike defense but not the expected path.

**#4 -- Adrian Luna Martinetti wins by finish**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Luna Martinetti has no UFC finishes and his recent fights have been very low-output decisions. Grant's chin has held up through a long career without a KO loss on record in this stretch. A finish for Martinetti would require a sequence that hasn't materialized yet against any opponent at this level.

<!--END-->

That's not reverse line movement, but it is a case where the market has moved significantly away from the fighter with the more logical case.

However, with the odds now at -122 for Grant, there's only a modest value window remaining from what would have been a cleaner edge at the opening line of +150.

## Raoni Barcelos -- Style & Profile

- At 39, Barcelos has quietly reinvented himself into a grappling-first decision machine. His career striking numbers (4.65 SLpM) don't reflect what he's actually doing lately — his last three fights show output of 0.2, 1.07, and 0.4 strikes per minute, meaning he's almost entirely abandoning the stand-up and winning on positional control and wrestling.
- His 87% takedown defense is the most important number on his sheet. Against a fighter like Jackson who needs to wrestle to neutralize strikers, Barcelos can pick his spots — and when he does shoot, he's accurate enough (31% on modest attempts) to control where the fight goes.
- He has not finished anyone by KO/TKO in his last 10 fights, and his lone recent submission came in Round 3 of a fight he was already winning comfortably. He wins by attrition, not by stoppage — and he's very good at it.
- The age concern is real. At 39, he's past the cliff for lighter divisions, and two historical weight misses signal a body that has been fighting its own weight class for years. Nothing in his recent run suggests dramatic physical decline yet, but it's the backdrop against which everything else is read.
- He already beat Jackson once, three weeks ago, by split decision — a close, grindy fight that came down to Barcelos doing just enough to steal rounds. That result is the single most relevant data point in this rematch.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Montel Jackson -- Style & Profile

- Jackson is built around two things that barely showed up in the first fight: his 8-inch reach advantage and his three first-round KO finishes in his last several years. He has real pop, but against Barcelos he landed less than one significant strike per minute and attempted zero takedowns. That's a complete shutdown performance by his opponent.
- His career striking absorption rate of 1.40 per minute is elite-level defensive output, which explains how he's navigated tough competition. But his last three fights have seen his offensive output crater — 0.27, 0.53, and 0.93 SLpM — suggesting that when opponents take away his range and force him to fight at close quarters, his offense disappears.
- His 67% takedown defense is the exploitable number. It's not bad, but it's not the 75%+ threshold that gives wrestlers real confidence. Against Barcelos, who rarely shoots but is accurate when he does, that number matters.
- He's now lost two straight by split decision — both fights where he appeared competitive but couldn't impose his preferred game. The Figueiredo loss came the same way: low output, passive, losing on control and activity. There's a pattern developing.
- One historical weight miss, which is less alarming than Barcelos's two, but at 34 in a 135-pound division, weight class management will bear watching in terms of stamina and sharpness late in fights.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The most important thing to understand about this rematch is that we already watched it happen. Barcelos neutralized Jackson's reach advantage by staying close, denied him clean exchanges from the outside, and outworked him for three rounds in a way that was ugly but effective. The split decision scorecards reflect how close the judges saw it, but Barcelos dictated the terms of the fight from start to finish. Jackson couldn't get to his power, couldn't establish his jab at range, and never once attempted a takedown despite his career averages suggesting he's capable of it.

The structural problem for Jackson going into a rematch is that his primary weapons — the reach, the knockout power at distance — require space that Barcelos specifically doesn't give him. Barcelos's 87% takedown defense means Jackson can't simply drag him to the mat if the striking isn't working, and Jackson's own grappling output in the first fight was zero. Barcelos, meanwhile, wins ugly: minimal output, clinch work, enough cage control to take rounds from judges who reward activity and aggression. That formula worked once. Nothing about Jackson's recent form suggests he's added a new wrinkle that breaks it.

The honest path to a Jackson victory is that three weeks is a very short turnaround for both men, and if anything, it might disrupt Barcelos's preparation more than Jackson's given the age gap. Jackson also has genuine one-punch power that could change the complexion of the fight immediately if Barcelos gets sloppy. But Jackson's passivity in the last two fights is the real warning sign — he's not a fighter who adapts mid-fight and finds a third option. If Barcelos clogs the angles again, Jackson doesn't have a demonstrated ability to problem-solve out of it. The net intangible difference favors Jackson slightly on age and freshness, but Barcelos has the psychological edge of already winning this exact fight.

Likelihood: High

**#2 — Jackson wins by decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: The first fight was a split decision, which means at least one judge thought Jackson did enough to win it. If Jackson is more aggressive early — using his reach to establish the jab before Barcelos closes the distance — he can steal rounds and get the nod on the cards. His power at range is legitimate, and Barcelos's minimalist approach sometimes leaves him vulnerable to fighters who simply impose their will on the outside.

**#3 — Jackson wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Jackson has finished three opponents in the first round in recent years, and he does carry genuine knockout power. If Barcelos gets caught being overly aggressive in a takedown attempt or walks into a clean left hand coming in, this fight ends early. It's a real possibility precisely because Barcelos has to enter Jackson's danger zone to execute his game plan.

**#4 — Barcelos wins by submission, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Barcelos averaged one submission attempt per 15 minutes in the first fight and has the grappling to threaten from top position if he gets a takedown late. His only recent sub came in Round 3 when he was already ahead and had full control. If he gets a clean takedown and Jackson fades late, he could finish it on the ground.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Ryan Spann -- Style & Profile

- Spann is a finisher first and foremost — across his last ten fights, he has zero decision wins. Every single victory has come by KO/TKO or submission, and five of those finishes came in Round 1. He hunts for the stoppage from the opening bell.
- His striking output is low by UFC light heavyweight standards (3.11 significant strikes per minute career, and even lower in his recent fights), but his accuracy has ticked up lately — he landed 55.6% against Buchecha in their first meeting and 42.1% against Brzeski, both above his career mark of 39%.
- He absorbs more strikes than he lands (3.92 absorbed per minute career), which is a real problem given his history. He has been knocked out or TKO'd in multiple recent fights — losses to Bogdan Guskov, Waldo Cortes Acosta, and Nikita Krylov all ended violently, and two of those three came in Round 2.
- His grappling is legitimate. He is averaging 1.7 submission attempts per 15 minutes over his career, and his recent submission over Brzeski showed he can mix in wrestling and capitalize from control positions. Against Buchecha specifically, he never attempted a takedown — he kept it standing and got the finish.
- At 34, he is approaching the age where absorbing damage starts compounding. The KO loss to Waldo Cortes Acosta in March 2025 and the prior loss to Guskov paint a picture of a fighter who is one clean shot away from an early night on any given evening.
- One weight miss in his history is a minor flag but not a pattern — not a major cardio concern for a three-round fight.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Marcus Buchecha -- Style & Profile

- Buchecha is one of the greatest Brazilian jiu-jitsu competitors in history, but that pedigree has not translated cleanly to MMA. He has zero finishes in his last three UFC fights — a KO loss, a draw, and a decision loss — and his submission average has dropped to 0.4 attempts per 15 minutes, nearly absent.
- His striking is sparse and unpolished. He is averaging just 1.59 significant strikes per minute career, and that number has cratered recently — 0.13 per minute against Nzechukwu and 0.56 per minute when he was knocked out by Spann. He simply does not throw enough to threaten a real striker.
- The 0% takedown defense across his UFC career is not a typo — it is a systemic problem. He has been taken down or controlled in fights where he could not impose his own grappling, and his 16% takedown accuracy means he is not converting his own attempts at any reliable rate either.
- He is 36 years old and has gone 0-2-1 across his last three fights, all at the highest level of competition his career has offered. The trend is not favorable, and the physical skills that made him elite on the jiu-jitsu mat — patience, positional grinding — require getting the fight to the ground first, which he has failed to do consistently.
- His best path in any fight is clinch pressure, body locks, and fence work to create takedown opportunities. When that fails, he is essentially a non-threatening striker eating damage on the outside, which is exactly what happened when Spann knocked him out in Round 2.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

These two already fought six days ago, which makes this rematch context almost unprecedented in modern UFC booking — both men are stepping back into the same fight with six days of preparation. Spann won that fight by TKO in Round 2, and the mechanics of how he did it tell you nearly everything you need to know about how this rematch plays out. Buchecha attempted takedowns at a reasonable rate (2.09 per 15 minutes) but his striking output was nearly nonexistent (0.56 SLpM), and Spann was able to stand in the pocket and land with accuracy on a fighter who defends only 50% of strikes coming at him. The fundamental problem for Buchecha has not changed in six days: he cannot threaten Spann on the feet, and if he cannot get the fight to the mat, he is absorbing punishment from a man with real finishing power.

Spann's own defensive vulnerability — absorbing almost four significant strikes per minute across his career, with a real history of getting stopped — is the wildcard. ## Eric McConico -- Style & Profile

- McConico is a southpaw who fights at a low volume and wins by doing just enough — he averaged under 1.5 significant strikes per minute across his last three fights, but his striking accuracy in those bouts was surprisingly high, hovering around 58–79%. He doesn't throw often, but when he does, he tends to connect.
- His best weapon is his reach. At 77 inches, he has a 4-inch advantage over Vieira, and he uses that to keep the fight at a distance where he can pick his shots without getting grabbed. Against Vieira specifically, he landed 78.6% of his strikes, which is an extraordinary number and suggests Vieira had serious trouble tracking his angles as a southpaw.
- Defensively, his 68% takedown defense is the number that matters most in this rematch. Vieira lives on the ground, and McConico's ability to stay upright is the single biggest variable in who wins this fight. In their first meeting, Vieira attempted takedowns at a 4.0 per 15 minutes rate and still lost the decision — meaning McConico either stuffed enough or scrambled back to his feet consistently.
- He has been knocked out twice in his recent history — by Nursulton Ruziboev in Round 2 and by Baisangur Susurkaev in Round 3. Both were later-round stoppages, which points more toward cardio-related chin erosion under accumulated pressure than a pure durability problem. Against striker-types, he is KO-able.
- At 35, he is right at the age where physical decline starts to quietly show up. His last-3 stats show a fighter who is conservative with his offense and relies on activity management rather than volume or power. He is unlikely to go out and blow anyone away — he grinds and maneuvers.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Rodolfo Vieira -- Style & Profile

- Vieira is a black belt-level jiu-jitsu specialist who has built his entire UFC career on putting fights on the floor. His 2.87 takedowns per 15 minutes and 0.9 submission attempts per 15 minutes define who he is — but his 22% takedown accuracy is alarmingly low for someone whose entire game depends on getting to the mat. He shoots often and misses a lot.
- His striking numbers are deceiving. A 54% striking accuracy sounds competent, but his 4.28 strikes absorbed per minute tells the real story — he gets hit frequently, his 49% strike defense is below average, and he tends to walk forward absorbing punishment while looking for the clinch or the level change. Against anyone with real striking, he takes damage.
- He has been finished three times in his last five fights. Bo Nickal knocked him out in Round 3, Anthony Hernandez submitted him in Round 2, and he lost decisions to Chris Curtis and Andre Petroski. He has struggled against wrestlers who can match his grappling and against strikers who can keep him at range. That is a wide vulnerability window.
- His submission wins have all come against opponents who gave him the scramble — he finished Saparbeg Safarov, Armen Petrosyan, Cody Brundage, and Dustin Stoltzfus, none of whom are elite grapplers. Against someone with real takedown defense, his submission game never gets activated.
- He is 36 and coming off a knockout loss, which is the kind of combination that rarely trends in a positive direction. His striking metrics in the Nickal fight — 0.56 strikes landed per minute at 36.8% accuracy — suggest he was completely overwhelmed on the feet and had no answer for it. That performance lingered into the McConico rematch where he again lost despite landing 4 takedowns per 15 minutes.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This is a rematch, which means we have a direct data point to anchor everything else — and McConico already won. The fundamental dynamic of this fight is simple: Vieira needs to get McConico down and keep him there long enough to work submissions, and McConico needs to stay upright and use his 4-inch reach advantage to control distance from the outside. In their first meeting, McConico won that battle convincingly enough to take a unanimous decision. Vieira attempted takedowns at an aggressive rate but couldn't impose himself consistently, and McConico's southpaw angles made him a difficult target from the outside.

The structural problem for Vieira is that his takedown accuracy sits at 22% for his career, and McConico's 68% takedown defense compounds that badly. Against a longer southpaw who is deliberately circling and using the reach, Vieira's level changes are slow enough that an opponent with decent TDD can time the sprawl. The blueprint already exists — McConico used it successfully six days ago. The deeper issue is that even when Vieira did get takedowns in the first fight, scoring at 4.0 per 15 minutes in a loss means position isn't translating to damage or submission attempts at a rate that wins rounds. McConico appears to be comfortable on the ground long enough to stand back up or survive control.

Where Vieira has a realistic path is the attrition game. McConico has two late-round KO losses on his record, and he operates at such low output that if Vieira can take enough strikes to stay in his face and drag the fight into a grinding third round, the accumulated pressure and some lucky positioning could flip the result. McConico also absorbed a third-round TKO loss to Susurkaev, suggesting the cardio question is real — though that loss came from a striker, not a grappler. The net intangible picture is fairly even: both fighters are 35–36, both fought six days ago, and neither has a meaningful edge in freshness or preparation. The rematch context slightly favors McConico, who has a proven counter to Vieira's game and will walk into this one with added confidence having just beaten him.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- McConico wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: High
Why: This is how the first fight ended and the structural reasons for it remain unchanged. McConico's reach advantage and southpaw stance neutralize Vieira's forward pressure, and Vieira's poor takedown accuracy means he can't drag the fight into the grappling exchange he needs. McConico out-points him on the feet across three rounds by doing just enough without engaging in the exchanges that get him hurt.

**#2 -- Vieira wins by submission, Round 2 or 3**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Vieira's entire career of submission finishes shows he can find the finish when he gets proper top position or a scramble opportunity. If McConico's cardio fades in the second or third round and Vieira catches a takedown in a clinch sequence, the jiu-jitsu pedigree is real enough to threaten. His sub attempt rate, even in a losing effort against McConico, was 1.0 per 15 minutes in fight one — he was still hunting.

**#3 -- Vieira wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Vieira can rack up enough takedown attempts and ground control even without finishing, judges sometimes reward the aggressor in a grappling-heavy fight. McConico won the first one, but the margins in decisions are rarely wide, and a judge-by-judge scenario where Vieira gets credited for shot volume and cage pressure is plausible — especially if McConico's output remains at the minimal levels it hit in the first meeting.

**#4 -- McConico wins by TKO, Round 2 or 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Vieira absorbs 4.28 significant strikes per minute for his career and his strike defense is below average. He walks into shots repeatedly. McConico isn't a power puncher, but a well-timed right hand down the middle on the southpaw-orthodox angle — the same angle that has given Vieira trouble — could wobble him if McConico finds confidence and opens up. This is the least likely scenario given McConico's low finishing rate, but it's not implausible given Vieira's durability record.

The structural problem for Vieira is that his takedown accuracy sits at 22% for his career, and McConico's 68% takedown defense compounds that badly. Against a longer southpaw who is deliberately circling and using the reach, Vieira's level changes are slow enough that an opponent with decent TDD can time the sprawl. The blueprint already exists — McConico used it successfully six days ago. The deeper issue is that even when Vieira did get takedowns in the first fight, scoring at 4.0 per 15 minutes in a loss means position isn't translating to damage or submission attempts at a rate that wins rounds. McConico appears to be comfortable on the ground long enough to stand back up or survive control.

Where Vieira has a realistic path is the attrition game. McConico has two late-round KO losses on his record, and he operates at such low output that if Vieira can take enough strikes to stay in his face and drag the fight into a grinding third round, the accumulated pressure and some lucky positioning could flip the result. McConico also absorbed a third-round TKO loss to Susurkaev, suggesting the cardio question is real — though that loss came from a striker, not a grappler. The net intangible picture is fairly even: both fighters are 35–36, both fought six days ago, and neither has a meaningful edge in freshness or preparation. The rematch context slightly favors McConico, who has a proven counter to Vieira's game and will walk into this one with added confidence having just beaten him.

<!--END-->

<!--ENDINGS-->
## Most Likely Fight Endings

Likelihood: High
Why: This is how the first fight ended and the structural reasons for it remain unchanged. McConico's reach advantage and southpaw stance neutralize Vieira's forward pressure, and Vieira's poor takedown accuracy means he can't drag the fight into the grappling exchange he needs. McConico out-points him on the feet across three rounds by doing just enough without engaging in the exchanges that get him hurt.

**#2 -- Vieira wins by submission, Round 2 or 3**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Vieira's entire career of submission finishes shows he can find the finish when he gets proper top position or a scramble opportunity. If McConico's cardio fades in the second or third round and Vieira catches a takedown in a clinch sequence, the jiu-jitsu pedigree is real enough to threaten. Likelihood: Low
Why: If Vieira can rack up enough takedown attempts and ground control even without finishing, judges sometimes reward the aggressor in a grappling-heavy fight. analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Jackson McVey -- Style & Profile

- McVey is a high-volume striker who throws at a blistering pace — 6.51 significant strikes per minute on his career average, and he hit 9.4 per minute in the first fight with Dumas. He wins by overwhelming opponents with output, not by setting traps or picking shots.
- His striking accuracy is genuinely elite for the weight class at 69% career, and he was even sharper in the rematch at 72.4%. That number tells you his output isn't desperation volume — he's connecting on the bulk of what he throws.
- The glaring vulnerability is his defense: 23% strike defense career-wide means roughly three in four shots the opponent throws are landing. He gets hit a lot. In a weight class full of power, that's a structural problem that will eventually matter more than it has so far.
- His grappling offense is essentially non-existent on paper — zero recorded takedowns — but he carries a 1.2 submission attempts per 15 minutes, and he finished Dumas with a submission in Round 1. His ground work appears opportunistic rather than systematic, likely coming from scrambles rather than planned wrestling.
- He's coming off two consecutive submission losses (Ferreira, Reese) before this win over Dumas, and those defeats came quickly — Round 1 at 3:35 and Round 2 at 1:38. When his striking doesn't overwhelm someone fast, he has shown he can get taken down and finished on the mat.
- At 27, he's on the ascending part of his career curve, and the line movement toward him suggests the market has bought into his performance against Dumas heavily. The question is whether that win exposed a specific Dumas weakness or reflected a true leap in McVey's ability.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Sedriques Dumas -- Style & Profile

- Dumas is a low-volume, defensively measured fighter who generates under 2 significant strikes per minute career-wide. He wins fights by grinding out decisions through attrition and consistent activity, not by threatening finishes — three of his last four wins came by unanimous decision.
- His strike defense at 53% is solid, but that number is being tested hard lately. He absorbed 1.34 strikes per minute against McVey and only landed 1.34 per minute himself — he was badly out-worked on the feet in that fight without producing much offense in return.
- He has been finished by submission in back-to-back fights now — tapped out to Donte Johnson in Round 2, then submitted by McVey in Round 1 of their first meeting. Before that, he was knocked out twice in his last five fights (Oleksiejczuk in R1, Ruziboev in R1). That's four finishes by stoppage in his last five completed fights, which is a serious red flag about his durability when things go wrong.
- His takedown game is limited: 31% accuracy on 1.3 attempts per 15 minutes career-wide, and he recorded zero takedowns in both recent submission losses. He's not using his wrestling as a tool to change the fight when he's in trouble.
- The reach advantage (79" vs. 77") gives him a marginal range edge on paper, but given how thoroughly he was out-struck in the first meeting, that structural advantage hasn't translated into output or control.
- At 30, Dumas is not over any significant age cliff, but his recent fight history paints a picture of a fighter who has been overwhelmed in every high-pressure moment over the last 18 months. He hasn't found an answer yet to opponents who push the pace on him.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The first meeting between these two settled a lot of questions. McVey walked Dumas down at 9.4 strikes per minute and finished him with a submission inside two and a half minutes of Round 1. Dumas managed 1.34 strikes per minute in return and zero takedowns — he couldn't get the fight to where he needed it and couldn't hold off McVey's pressure on the feet. The structural problem for Dumas is straightforward: McVey is simply a faster, higher-output striker, and Dumas has no reliable answer either on the feet or on the ground. Dumas' decision wins earlier in his career came against fighters who let him control pace and distance; against someone who takes the cage away from him, he has no Plan B.

McVey's own vulnerabilities are real but less relevant in this specific matchup. His 23% strike defense would be terrifying against a power puncher, and he's been submitted twice himself. But Dumas is not a sub specialist — he averages 0.2 submission attempts per 15 minutes career-wide — and Dumas hasn't been the aggressor in any of his recent fights. McVey can afford to eat some strikes in the early going because Dumas isn't threatening to close the show with one punch or one choke. The path that burns McVey tends to involve opponents who pressure him on the ground (Ferreira, Reese), which is precisely what Dumas has been unable to do.

The net intangible picture is essentially neutral — both fighters are six days removed from their last fight, which was the first meeting between them. Neither has a layoff concern, and without additional camp or weight cut data, there's no meaningful adjustment to apply there. What the line movement tells us is that sharp money has moved significantly toward McVey since the opening, shortening him from -150 to -210. That move aligns cleanly with the performance data: anyone who watched the first fight saw a dominant McVey performance, and the market has repriced accordingly. The path to a Dumas win requires either a dramatically different game plan that neutralizes McVey's pace or McVey gassing in a way his fight history doesn't predict over three rounds. Neither scenario has much concrete evidence behind it.
analysis_sections.endings:

Likelihood: High

Dumas has been finished by ground and pound or submission in back-to-back fights now with zero apparent correction, and McVey is the aggressor who forces those situations.

**#2 -- Jackson McVey wins by TKO or decision, Round 2 or later**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: If Dumas survives the opening onslaught through better early defense or ring movement, McVey can still win by attrition — he out-strikes Dumas at nearly a 7-to-1 pace when both fighters' last-3 averages are compared. Dumas' 53% strike defense slows McVey's accuracy enough that a late finish or lopsided decision remains a realistic path.

**#3 -- Sedriques Dumas wins by decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Dumas' only realistic path is to neutralize McVey's pace early, establish some kind of grappling control he hasn't shown recently, and grind out rounds. His three career wins via decision show the template exists, but those wins came against fighters who didn't pressure him the way McVey does. The two recent submission losses suggest he's not executing that patient game plan under duress right now.

**#4 -- Sedriques Dumas wins by submission**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Dumas averaged 0.2 submission attempts per 15 minutes career-wide, and in both recent fights he posted zero. He would need to get McVey to the ground (McVey has been submitted before, so the vulnerability exists) but Dumas has shown no ability to initiate that sequence against an active, upright opponent in recent outings. The scenario requires him to do something he has failed to do in his last two UFC appearances.

## Michelle Montague -- Style & Profile

- Undefeated at 8-0, but her UFC sample is limited to two fights — both unanimous decisions against Mayra Bueno Silva and Luana Carolina. She wins by outworking opponents over three rounds rather than hunting the finish; her last two bouts ended in decision with zero KO or submission attempts recorded.
- Her striking output has been unusually low in UFC competition — under 1.1 significant strikes per minute in her most recent fight and 0.67 in the one before — but her accuracy has been high (80% against Bueno Silva). She's a selective striker who doesn't throw to throw.
- Her takedown numbers are modest in UFC (3.5 per 15 minutes, 53% accuracy), but she has consistently used them to control fights and secure decisions. She doesn't look for submissions off her back or from top position — zero sub attempts in UFC.
- As a southpaw, she presents a less familiar look, and the left-hand straight and left kick from that stance are natural disruptors against orthodox fighters. There's no recent southpaw history on the other side of this matchup to assess how much that matters.
- Her strike defense is a genuine concern — 41% is low, meaning she absorbs a significant share of what's thrown at her. Against a live UFC-level striker this would be a red flag, but in this specific matchup context it matters much less.
- Her layoff listed as six days is almost certainly a data error and likely reflects a recent fight date in the dataset rather than an actual six-day turnaround. Treating her as a normally prepared fighter unless contradicted by other information.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Assuerio Silva -- Style & Profile

- Assuerio Silva is 51 years old and has not competed since April 2007 — a layoff of approximately 19 years. This is not ring rust in any conventional sense. This is a fighter returning from nearly two decades of inactivity, which makes every stat from his UFC career essentially historical artifact rather than current predictive data.
- His last three UFC fights were all losses — a majority decision loss to Cheick Kongo, a first-round submission loss to Brandon Vera, and a unanimous decision loss to Tim Sylvia. He was being outclassed by the heavyweight division's middle tier in 2006–2007.
- His UFC striking output was minimal (1.42 sig strikes per minute career average) and he absorbed 4.20 per minute — nearly three times what he put out. He was a grappling-first fighter who struggled badly whenever fights stayed on the feet.
- His takedown accuracy of 34% and takedown defense of just 25% were both below average even for the standards of his era. He was not an elite grappler; his submission wins came early in fights against lesser opposition, not against UFC-caliber opponents.
- At a listed weight of 241 lbs, he is a heavyweight attempting to compete against a 135-lb women's bantamweight. This is a novelty or exhibition booking — the weight differential alone (over 100 lbs) makes this one of the most structurally unusual matchups that can be entered into any analysis system.
- His age at 51, combined with 19 years of inactivity and three straight losses at the end of his UFC run, means there is no realistic analytical framework in which his historical UFC stats carry predictive value for this fight.

## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This matchup is genuinely outside the bounds of normal MMA analysis. The competitors are separated by 19 years of age, over 100 pounds of body weight, and Assuerio Silva has not competed in any recorded capacity since April 2007. analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Cody Durden -- Style & Profile

- Durden is a grinder who wins through volume wrestling and relentless pressure rather than finishing power. His career striking output (3.24 sig. strikes/min) is modest, but his takedown average of 3.99 per 15 minutes is genuinely elite for flyweight — he gets fights where he wants them.
- The last three fights tell a concerning story of declining output. Against Tumendemberel he landed just 0.27 strikes per minute and managed only 3 takedowns per 15 minutes. Against Nascimento he was more active (4.38 SLpM) but got submitted in Round 2. The April win over Filho came on just 0.73 SLpM and 2.0 TD/15 — a control-based grind, not a dominant performance.
- His recent record is alarming: four losses in five fights before the Filho win, including back-to-back KO losses (Bruno Silva, Jose Ochoa) and a submission to Nascimento. The KO losses are worth flagging — Silva and Ochoa both stopped him in Round 2, suggesting he has been cracked at this level of competition. His strike defense sits at just 50% career, and he absorbed 3.94 sig. strikes per minute across his UFC run.
- At 35 years old, Durden is on the wrong side of the age curve for a flyweight. He has been stopped by knockout twice in his last seven fights, submitted twice more, and had multiple losses to unranked opponents. The body of evidence points toward a fighter in the late-career fade zone.
- His path to victory in any fight runs almost entirely through wrestling — if he can't consistently get the fight to the mat and hold top position, he has very little striking offense to fall back on. His 0 KO finishes in his last 10 fights confirms he is not a threat to end it standing.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Jafel Filho -- Style & Profile

- Filho is a submission specialist first and foremost. His career submission attempt rate of 1.8 per 15 minutes is high for flyweight, and four of his seven most recent wins have come by finish — three by submission and one by KO/TKO. He is not a man who wants to be on his feet trading.
- His striking numbers are the lowest on the card by any reasonable measure: 1.78 sig. strikes landed per minute career, 40% accuracy, and only 1.76 absorbed per minute — meaning fights involving Filho are slow, grinding, grappling-centered affairs where very little striking actually lands. When he won against Clayton Carpenter, he landed 1.06 strikes per minute; against Cody Durden in the most recent fight, just 0.2. These are among the lowest activity numbers you will see in professional MMA.
- His submission wins have all come in Round 1, which raises an important question about what happens when he can't finish quickly. His loss to Muhammad Mokaev was a Round 3 submission, his loss to Allan Nascimento was a decision after three rounds — both fights where he went deep into the tank and came up short. His cardio late in fights is a legitimate concern.
- His takedown defense is a real weakness at 50% career. Against a high-volume wrestler like Durden, who averages nearly four takedowns per 15 minutes, that number becomes the central variable in this matchup. If Durden gets to his wrestling, Filho has historically been dragged into grind fights he is not built to win.
- The April 2026 loss to Durden — his most recent fight — showed Filho at his absolute worst. He landed 0.2 strikes per minute, connected on just 25% of his strikes, and couldn't impose his submission game at all. That was not a competitive performance; it was a shutout.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This is a rematch, and the first fight already answered most of the structural questions. Durden is the better wrestler and the more active fighter. He grinds, he controls, he takes rounds on volume even when the action is sparse. Filho is the more dangerous submission finisher, but only if he can either drag Durden into scrambles or survive long enough to capitalize on a mistake. The problem for Filho is that his takedown defense at 50% is too porous to stop a wrestler averaging four takedowns per 15 minutes — and once Durden gets top position, he doesn't give it back. The first fight was essentially decided by that dynamic: Durden controlled position for three rounds, Filho couldn't generate the scrambles he needed, and the decision went wide.

Where Filho's case rests entirely is on Durden's durability and late-career red flags. Durden has been knocked out twice in his last seven fights and submitted twice more — he is stoppable. If Filho can pull guard, hunt a submission from bottom, or catch Durden in a scramble after a takedown attempt, he has the finishing tools to end it. The wildcard is that Filho's submission attempts were completely absent in their first meeting (0.0 sub attempts/15 min), which suggests Durden smothered him effectively and never gave him the space to work. For Filho to reverse that outcome, he needs to fight a fundamentally different fight than the one we already saw.

The net intangible picture slightly favors Filho — he's three years younger at 32 versus Durden's 35, and youth is a real factor for a fighter like Durden who has been KO'd and submitted multiple times recently. But the first fight is the loudest data point in this rematch, and it wasn't close. Durden's wrestling neutralized everything Filho does well, and there is no obvious reason that changes on six days' rest for a rematch.
analysis_sections.endings:

Likelihood: High
Why: The first fight set a clear blueprint. Durden's wrestling volume gives him consistent control over where the fight takes place, and Filho has not shown the takedown defense to stop it. Three rounds of top control and grinding pace play directly to Durden's strengths, and his decision win record (3 decisions in last 10 fights) supports this as his most reliable path.

If he can pull guard early, catch Durden off a sloppy takedown attempt, or lure him into a scramble, he has the submission skills to capitalize. Durden has been submitted twice in his last five fights, so the vulnerability is real — but Filho could not threaten once in their first meeting.

Likelihood: Low
Why: Durden does carry a submission average of 0.7 attempts per 15 minutes and has one submission finish in his last 10 fights. If Filho gases out chasing a finish that never comes and Durden senses his opponent fading, a late submission opportunity is not impossible — but Durden's finishing rate is low enough to make this a distant third option.

**#4 -- Jafel Filho wins by decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Filho has won by decision only sporadically and his output in the last fight was so low (0.2 SLpM) that winning three rounds on the scorecards against an active wrestler seems implausible without a significantly different game plan. A path exists if Filho manages to stuff takedowns consistently and work volume from the outside, but his 50% TDD career number makes that scenario unlikely to materialize over three full rounds.
